_meta.notes: Which bureau each issuer pulls. Varies by state (CA/FL get Experian preference for some; TX often Equifax). Knowing the bureau lets you sequence apps to spread inquiries across bureaus.

chase.business_pull: personal hard pull (Chase Ink/Business pulls personal credit)
chase.notes: Chase rarely pulls Equifax. Most users see Experian.

amex.personal_pull: single hard pull (sometimes none if existing relationship)
amex.business_pull: soft pull for many existing Amex customers; hard for new
amex.notes: Amex famously soft-pulls when you have an existing relationship. New cardholders almost always hard pull.

citi.notes: Sometimes pulls Experian instead — DPs vary.

capital_one.personal_pull: TRIPLE hard pull (TU, EQ, EX simultaneously)
capital_one.notes: Cap One is unique: pulls all three bureaus on every application. One Cap One app = three inquiries. This is why churning Cap One is generally not worth it.

wells_fargo.business_pull: single hard pull (does NOT report to personal bureaus though)

discover.business_pull: single hard pull (DOES report to personal bureaus, counts toward 5/24)

tactical_use.spread_inquiries_across_bureaus: If app-day with 3 cards: Chase (EX) + Citi (EQ) + BoA (TU) = one hard inquiry per bureau, none compounded.
tactical_use.minimize_total_inquiries: Chase + Amex same day = both EX. Two inquiries on Experian, none on EQ/TU. May matter if applying for mortgage soon and you want EX clean.
tactical_use.freeze_unused_bureaus: If all your applications hit one bureau (e.g., BoA-heavy = TU), you can freeze the other two bureaus for fraud protection without affecting your CC apps. ChexSystems is separate.
